item.title: DBSegFormer: A Dual-Branch Framework based on Multi-scale Information Fusion for Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Fenger Li
He Yan
Maocheng Zhao
Liang Qi
Liyong Fu
Qiaolin Ye
item.prism:publicationName: Information Fusion
item.description: This study introduced DBSegFormer, an efficient multi-scale fusion framework for semantic segmentation. It features a dual-branch Transformer architecture, where the main branch uses SegFormer’s MixVision Transformer for global semantic learning, and a convolutional branch captures fine-grained details like textures and edges. The Multi-Scale Attention Fusion (MSAF) module combines content- and scale-adaptive features using dilated convolutions and gated attention, improving detail preservation. The fused features are processed by a lightweight MLP decoder, avoiding complex up-sampling and enhancing efficiency. Experiments demonstrate significant performance improvements over CNN/Transformer baselines, with efficient memory and latency usage, and strong zero-shot generalization across distribution shifts, making DBSegFormer a promising approach for high-precision, resource-efficient segmentation. Semantic segmentation assigns a semantic label to every pixel and is crucial for detailed scene understanding. The core challenge is to capture long-range global context while preserving high-resolution local details needed for precise boundaries, without relying on heavy decoders. We propose DBSegFormer, an efficient framework-based on multi-scale information fusion that combines a dual-branch Transformer backbone with a lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) decoder. The design embodies two key innovations: (i) a dual-branch architecture in which the main branch employs SegFormer’s MixVision Transformer to learn multi-scale global semantics, while a lightweight convolutional branch focuses on fine-grained details (e.g., textures, edges, small objects) often degraded by deep down-sampling; and (ii) a Multi-Scale Attention Fusion (MSAF) module that performs content- and scale-adaptive integration via multi-scale dilated convolutions and gated attention, yielding complementary, detail-aware representations. The fused features are then aggregated by a minimal MLP head, avoiding complex up-sampling heads and improving efficiency and stability. Extensive experiments across multiple public benchmarks show consistent gains over strong CNN/Transformer baselines while maintaining a favorable trade-off between segmentation accuracy and computational cost. These results indicate that decoupling semantic abstraction from detail retention and reunifying them through adaptive multi-scale fusion provides a simple yet effective solution for high-precision semantic segmentation. Published: 2026-05-07T16:08:37+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.832 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fenger Li; He Yan; Maocheng Zhao; Liang Qi; Liyong Fu; Qiaolin Ye

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104454

Relevance: 0.832 (must_read)

This study introduced DBSegFormer, an efficient multi-scale fusion framework for semantic segmentation. It features a dual-branch Transformer architecture, where the main branch uses SegFormer’s MixVision Transformer for global semantic learning, and a convolutional branch captures fine-grained details like textures and edges. The Multi-Scale Attention Fusion (MSAF) module combines content- and scale-adaptive features using dilated convolutions and gated attention, improving detail preservation. The fused features are processed by a lightweight MLP decoder, avoiding complex up-sampling and enhancing efficiency. Experiments demonstrate significant performance improvements over CNN/Transformer baselines, with efficient memory and latency usage, and strong zero-shot generalization across distribution shifts, making DBSegFormer a promising approach for high-precision, resource-efficient segmentation. Semantic segmentation assigns a semantic label to every pixel and is crucial for detailed scene understanding. The core challenge is to capture long-range global context while preserving high-resolution local details needed for precise boundaries, without relying on heavy decoders. We propose DBSegFormer, an efficient framework-based on multi-scale information fusion that combines a dual-branch Transformer backbone with a lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) decoder. The design embodies two key innovations: (i) a dual-branch architecture in which the main branch employs SegFormer’s MixVision Transformer to learn multi-scale global semantics, while a lightweight convolutional branch focuses on fine-grained details (e.g., textures, edges, small objects) often degraded by deep down-sampling; and (ii) a Multi-Scale Attention Fusion (MSAF) module that performs content- and scale-adaptive integration via multi-scale dilated convolutions and gated attention, yielding complementary, detail-aware representations. The fused features are then aggregated by a minimal MLP head, avoiding complex up-sampling heads and improving efficiency and stability. Extensive experiments across multiple public benchmarks show consistent gains over strong CNN/Transformer baselines while maintaining a favorable trade-off between segmentation accuracy and computational cost. These results indicate that decoupling semantic abstraction from detail retention and reunifying them through adaptive multi-scale fusion provides a simple yet effective solution for high-precision semantic segmentation.

item.title: MIST: A Benchmark and Baseline for Multi-frame Infrared Small Target Detection in Complex Motion
item.dc:creator: Rui Gao
Meihong Zhang
Gongyang Li
Guanyi Li
Kai Zhao
Xianchao Zhang
Dan Zeng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Motion cues play a vital role in multi-frame infrared small target detection (MISTD). However, most targets in existing datasets exhibit regular and slow motion, which cannot reflect the complex and diverse motion patterns in real-world scenarios. This biased data distribution makes recent data-driven methods highly rely on simplified motion assumptions that tend to fail in irregular or fast motion, resulting in noisy feature representations cluttered with target-irrelevant factors. Hence, we stress that methods for MISTD should also work when targets are in complex motion. To enable this research, we propose a large-scale dataset called MIST for airborne infrared detection scenarios. The dataset is built on a synthetic data engine that models variations in pose, size, and intensity of moving targets while seamlessly blending them into real backgrounds for physical, geometric, and visual realism. Targets in MIST exhibit low signal-to-clutter ratios and complex motion, making it a promising yet challenging benchmark for developing algorithms focused on motion analysis. To tackle the challenges of MIST, we develop MISTNet, a robust baseline based on the Information Bottleneck theory. To handle irregular and fast motion, we propose a shifted neighborhood compensation block to efficiently model multi-scale correspondences for implicit motion compensation. To distill compact representations free from irrelevant cues, we design a progressive distillation decoder to hierarchically filter out redundancy while preserving target-relevant information. We benchmark 31 state-of-the-art methods and find that their performance on MIST drops significantly compared with that on the widely used NUDT-MIRSDT dataset. Our MISTNet outperforms all other methods by a large margin, with an over 6% gain in the IoU metric, demonstrating its superiority. The dataset, code, and model weights are available at https://github.com/GR-ray/MIST. Published: 2026-05-06T19:43:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.830 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rui Gao; Meihong Zhang; Gongyang Li; Guanyi Li; Kai Zhao; Xianchao Zhang; Dan Zeng

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3689420

Relevance: 0.830 (must_read)

Motion cues play a vital role in multi-frame infrared small target detection (MISTD). However, most targets in existing datasets exhibit regular and slow motion, which cannot reflect the complex and diverse motion patterns in real-world scenarios. This biased data distribution makes recent data-driven methods highly rely on simplified motion assumptions that tend to fail in irregular or fast motion, resulting in noisy feature representations cluttered with target-irrelevant factors. Hence, we stress that methods for MISTD should also work when targets are in complex motion. To enable this research, we propose a large-scale dataset called MIST for airborne infrared detection scenarios. The dataset is built on a synthetic data engine that models variations in pose, size, and intensity of moving targets while seamlessly blending them into real backgrounds for physical, geometric, and visual realism. Targets in MIST exhibit low signal-to-clutter ratios and complex motion, making it a promising yet challenging benchmark for developing algorithms focused on motion analysis. To tackle the challenges of MIST, we develop MISTNet, a robust baseline based on the Information Bottleneck theory. To handle irregular and fast motion, we propose a shifted neighborhood compensation block to efficiently model multi-scale correspondences for implicit motion compensation. To distill compact representations free from irrelevant cues, we design a progressive distillation decoder to hierarchically filter out redundancy while preserving target-relevant information. We benchmark 31 state-of-the-art methods and find that their performance on MIST drops significantly compared with that on the widely used NUDT-MIRSDT dataset. Our MISTNet outperforms all other methods by a large margin, with an over 6% gain in the IoU metric, demonstrating its superiority. The dataset, code, and model weights are available at https://github.com/GR-ray/MIST.

item.title: Na-IRSTD: Enhancing Infrared Small Target Detection via Native-Resolution Feature Selection and Fusion
item.dc:creator: Qian Xu
Chi Zhang
Qiming Zhang
Xi Li
Haojuan Yuan
Mingjin Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces the inherent challenge of precisely localizing dim targets amid complex background clutter. While progress has been made, existing methods usually follow conventional strategies to downsample features and discard small targets' details, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we present Na-IRSTD, a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for IRSTD. This framework elegantly incorporates native-resolution features to preserve subtle target cues, overcoming the resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches and significantly improving the model's ability to localize small targets. We also introduce an effective token reduction and selection strategy, which selects target patches with high accuracy and confidence, boosting the low-level details of the feature while effectively reducing native-resolution patch tokens compared to dense processing, thereby avoiding imposing an unbearable computational burden. Extensive experiments demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our token reduction and selection strategy across multiple public datasets. Ultimately, our Na-IRSTD model achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks. Published: 2026-05-07T07:42:03+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.818 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qian Xu; Chi Zhang; Qiming Zhang; Xi Li; Haojuan Yuan; Mingjin Zhang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.818 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces the inherent challenge of precisely localizing dim targets amid complex background clutter. While progress has been made, existing methods usually follow conventional strategies to downsample features and discard small targets' details, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we present Na-IRSTD, a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for IRSTD. This framework elegantly incorporates native-resolution features to preserve subtle target cues, overcoming the resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches and significantly improving the model's ability to localize small targets. We also introduce an effective token reduction and selection strategy, which selects target patches with high accuracy and confidence, boosting the low-level details of the feature while effectively reducing native-resolution patch tokens compared to dense processing, thereby avoiding imposing an unbearable computational burden. Extensive experiments demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our token reduction and selection strategy across multiple public datasets. Ultimately, our Na-IRSTD model achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks.

item.title: SCASeg: Strip Cross-Attention for Efficient Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Guoan Xu
Jiaming Chen
Wenfeng Huang
Wenjing Jia
Guangwei Gao
Guo-Jun Qi
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) has achieved notable success in computer vision, with its variants widely validated across various downstream tasks, including semantic segmentation. However, as general-purpose visual encoders, ViT back-bones often do not fully address the specific requirements of task decoders, highlighting opportunities for designing decoders optimized for efficient semantic segmentation. This paper proposes Strip Cross-Attention (SCASeg), an innovative decoder head specifically designed for semantic segmentation. Instead of relying on the conventional skip connections, we utilize lateral connections between encoder and decoder stages, leveraging encoder features as Queries in cross-attention modules. Additionally, we introduce a Cross-Layer Block (CLB) that integrates hierarchical feature maps from various encoder and decoder stages to form a unified representation for Keys and Values. The CLB also incorporates the local perceptual strengths of convolution, enabling SCASeg to capture both global and local context dependencies across multiple layers, thus enhancing feature interaction at different scales and improving overall efficiency. To further optimize computational efficiency, SCASeg compresses the channels of queries and keys into one dimension, creating strip-like patterns that reduce memory usage and increase inference speed compared to traditional vanilla cross-attention. Experiments show that SCASeg’s adaptable decoder delivers competitive performance across various setups, outperforming leading segmentation architectures on benchmark datasets, including ADE20K, Cityscapes, COCO-Stuff 164k, and Pascal VOC2012, even under diverse computational constraints. Published: 2026-05-06T19:43:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.817 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guoan Xu; Jiaming Chen; Wenfeng Huang; Wenjing Jia; Guangwei Gao; Guo-Jun Qi

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3688157

Relevance: 0.817 (must_read)

The Vision Transformer (ViT) has achieved notable success in computer vision, with its variants widely validated across various downstream tasks, including semantic segmentation. However, as general-purpose visual encoders, ViT back-bones often do not fully address the specific requirements of task decoders, highlighting opportunities for designing decoders optimized for efficient semantic segmentation. This paper proposes Strip Cross-Attention (SCASeg), an innovative decoder head specifically designed for semantic segmentation. Instead of relying on the conventional skip connections, we utilize lateral connections between encoder and decoder stages, leveraging encoder features as Queries in cross-attention modules. Additionally, we introduce a Cross-Layer Block (CLB) that integrates hierarchical feature maps from various encoder and decoder stages to form a unified representation for Keys and Values. The CLB also incorporates the local perceptual strengths of convolution, enabling SCASeg to capture both global and local context dependencies across multiple layers, thus enhancing feature interaction at different scales and improving overall efficiency. To further optimize computational efficiency, SCASeg compresses the channels of queries and keys into one dimension, creating strip-like patterns that reduce memory usage and increase inference speed compared to traditional vanilla cross-attention. Experiments show that SCASeg’s adaptable decoder delivers competitive performance across various setups, outperforming leading segmentation architectures on benchmark datasets, including ADE20K, Cityscapes, COCO-Stuff 164k, and Pascal VOC2012, even under diverse computational constraints.

item.title: DSRF-SAR: Neural Radiance Field Representation Based on a Dual SAR Scattering Mechanism
item.dc:creator: Yang Yang
Yueting Zhang
Jiayi Guo
Guangyao Zhou
Xia Ning
Yongchao Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) enables high-resolution imaging of the Earth's surface under all weather and illumination conditions. However, the high cost of data acquisition and the limited diversity of viewpoints restrict its application in data-intensive tasks. Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) can implicitly represent scenes and synthesize novel-view images from unseen viewpoints. Existing NeRF-based methods have achieved promising results in SAR image generation. However, they often fail to capture the complex electromagnetic scattering characteristics and sharp geometric transitions. This limitation often results in blurring of target regions and the omission of background textures. To address these issues, we propose DSRF-SAR, a neural radiance field representation based on a dual SAR scattering mechanism. Specifically, the scattering is decomposed into two components: isotropic scattering, which depends solely on spatial location, and anisotropic scattering, which depends on both location and observation geometry. Additionally, directional interpolation is reparameterized by using the reflection direction of radar wave instead of the incident direction to learn a more accurate SAR scattering distribution. And the target surface is represented using a Signed Distance Function (SDF), enabling stronger geometric constraint and higher-fidelity scene reconstruction. Qualitative and quantitative experiments, including sparse-view interpolation and few-shot extrapolation, are conducted on the Moving and Stationary Target Acquisition and Recognition (MSTAR) dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches and generates high-quality SAR images of novel views, providing additional support for SAR image interpretation and applications. Published: 2026-05-06T19:41:24+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.814 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yang Yang; Yueting Zhang; Jiayi Guo; Guangyao Zhou; Xia Ning; Yongchao Zhao

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3690329

Relevance: 0.814 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) enables high-resolution imaging of the Earth's surface under all weather and illumination conditions. However, the high cost of data acquisition and the limited diversity of viewpoints restrict its application in data-intensive tasks. Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) can implicitly represent scenes and synthesize novel-view images from unseen viewpoints. Existing NeRF-based methods have achieved promising results in SAR image generation. However, they often fail to capture the complex electromagnetic scattering characteristics and sharp geometric transitions. This limitation often results in blurring of target regions and the omission of background textures. To address these issues, we propose DSRF-SAR, a neural radiance field representation based on a dual SAR scattering mechanism. Specifically, the scattering is decomposed into two components: isotropic scattering, which depends solely on spatial location, and anisotropic scattering, which depends on both location and observation geometry. Additionally, directional interpolation is reparameterized by using the reflection direction of radar wave instead of the incident direction to learn a more accurate SAR scattering distribution. And the target surface is represented using a Signed Distance Function (SDF), enabling stronger geometric constraint and higher-fidelity scene reconstruction. Qualitative and quantitative experiments, including sparse-view interpolation and few-shot extrapolation, are conducted on the Moving and Stationary Target Acquisition and Recognition (MSTAR) dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches and generates high-quality SAR images of novel views, providing additional support for SAR image interpretation and applications.

item.title: Image Fusion Based on Prior Information
item.dc:creator: Congbing He
Zhenhong Jia
Hui Zhao
Sensen Song
Jiajia Wang
Fei Shi
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Existing feature extraction-based image fusion methods have an initial output of noise and iteratively refine them toward the target through training. This approach results in slow convergence and unstable training, with suboptimal fusion quality. To address these limitations, we propose prior-based fusion (PBF), a novel paradigm that constructs the fused output using multi-source image priors as its structural foundation, unlike noise-initialized approaches. This strategy enables faster and more stable training, while allowing the model to focus on complementary information exploration and detail enhancement, ultimately achieving superior fusion quality. Building upon the PBF paradigm, we propose PBFNet, a general-purpose image fusion framework, with its unified version PBFNet-U. They use a manually designed fusion criterion as the switching function to achieve optimal prior information extraction with only a slight increase in model complexity. The novel dense residual dense block is used as a feature extraction block, and at the same time creates a continuous memory between convolutional blocks and convolutional layers, which has a stronger feature extraction capability than residual block, dense block, residual dense block, and aggregated residual dense block. Furthermore, a channel attention mechanism is incorporated to adaptively fuse encoded features, and a gradient-enhanced loss function is designed to improve gradient perception. Both quantitative metrics and visual assessments demonstrate that PBFNet and its unified version surpass state-of-the-art methods across four fusion tasks. The results of ablation studies and discussion and analysis demonstrate the significant advantages of the proposed paradigm and method in stabilizing training, accelerating convergence, and improving fusion performance. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/hcb5206/PBFNet. Published: 2026-05-06T19:40:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Congbing He; Zhenhong Jia; Hui Zhao; Sensen Song; Jiajia Wang; Fei Shi

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3690497

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

Existing feature extraction-based image fusion methods have an initial output of noise and iteratively refine them toward the target through training. This approach results in slow convergence and unstable training, with suboptimal fusion quality. To address these limitations, we propose prior-based fusion (PBF), a novel paradigm that constructs the fused output using multi-source image priors as its structural foundation, unlike noise-initialized approaches. This strategy enables faster and more stable training, while allowing the model to focus on complementary information exploration and detail enhancement, ultimately achieving superior fusion quality. Building upon the PBF paradigm, we propose PBFNet, a general-purpose image fusion framework, with its unified version PBFNet-U. They use a manually designed fusion criterion as the switching function to achieve optimal prior information extraction with only a slight increase in model complexity. The novel dense residual dense block is used as a feature extraction block, and at the same time creates a continuous memory between convolutional blocks and convolutional layers, which has a stronger feature extraction capability than residual block, dense block, residual dense block, and aggregated residual dense block. Furthermore, a channel attention mechanism is incorporated to adaptively fuse encoded features, and a gradient-enhanced loss function is designed to improve gradient perception. Both quantitative metrics and visual assessments demonstrate that PBFNet and its unified version surpass state-of-the-art methods across four fusion tasks. The results of ablation studies and discussion and analysis demonstrate the significant advantages of the proposed paradigm and method in stabilizing training, accelerating convergence, and improving fusion performance. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/hcb5206/PBFNet.

item.title: Cross-domain Few-shot Object Detection with Multi-modal Textual Enrichment
item.dc:creator: Zeyu Shangguan
Daniel Seita
Mohammad Rostami
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Advancements in cross-modal feature extraction and integration have significantly enhanced performance in few-shot learning tasks. However, current multi-modal object detection (MM-OD) methods often experience notable performance degradation when encountering substantial domain shifts. We propose that incorporating rich textual information can enable the model to establish a more robust knowledge relationship between visual instances and their corresponding language descriptions, thereby mitigating the challenges of domain shift. Specifically, we focus on the problem of Cross-Domain Multi-Modal Few-Shot Object Detection (CDMM-FSOD) and introduce a meta-learning-based framework designed to leverage rich textual semantics as an auxiliary modality to achieve effective domain adaptation. Our new architecture incorporates two key components: (i) A multi-modal feature aggregation module, which aligns visual and linguistic feature embeddings to ensure cohesive integration across modalities. (ii) A rich text semantic rectification module, which employs bidirectional text feature generation to refine multi-modal feature alignment, thereby enhancing understanding of language and its application in object detection. We evaluate the proposed method on three cross-domain object detection benchmarks and demonstrate that it significantly surpasses existing few-shot object detection approaches. The implementation of our method is publicly accessible at: https://github.com/zshanggu/CDMM. . Published: 2026-05-07T19:16:13+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zeyu Shangguan; Daniel Seita; Mohammad Rostami

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02839-7

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Advancements in cross-modal feature extraction and integration have significantly enhanced performance in few-shot learning tasks. However, current multi-modal object detection (MM-OD) methods often experience notable performance degradation when encountering substantial domain shifts. We propose that incorporating rich textual information can enable the model to establish a more robust knowledge relationship between visual instances and their corresponding language descriptions, thereby mitigating the challenges of domain shift. Specifically, we focus on the problem of Cross-Domain Multi-Modal Few-Shot Object Detection (CDMM-FSOD) and introduce a meta-learning-based framework designed to leverage rich textual semantics as an auxiliary modality to achieve effective domain adaptation. Our new architecture incorporates two key components: (i) A multi-modal feature aggregation module, which aligns visual and linguistic feature embeddings to ensure cohesive integration across modalities. (ii) A rich text semantic rectification module, which employs bidirectional text feature generation to refine multi-modal feature alignment, thereby enhancing understanding of language and its application in object detection. We evaluate the proposed method on three cross-domain object detection benchmarks and demonstrate that it significantly surpasses existing few-shot object detection approaches. The implementation of our method is publicly accessible at: https://github.com/zshanggu/CDMM. .

item.title: Folding the Vision: Towards Efficient Global Context Representation on Edge Devices
item.dc:creator: Ang Li
Shouxiang Ni
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Traditional efficient CNNs face limitations in capturing global dependencies due to local receptive fields, while ViTs suffer from quadratic complexity. Recent works attempt to combine convolution and attention mechanisms, yet still struggle with computational overhead that limits inference speed on mobile devices. In this work, we propose FoldNet, a pure convolutional architecture that achieves global context representation through hardware-efficient operations. First, we propose the Fold Convolution that reorganizes spatial pixels into the channel dimension, capturing global spatial attention via channel interactions, with lower computational costs compared to ViTs. Second, we design the Partial Re-parameterization Module that applies the structural re-parameterization technique to a subset of feature channels. This approach achieves nearly identical performance compared to full-channel processing while significantly minimizing redundant computations and memory accesses. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of FoldNet over existing state-of-the-art methods. For example, on ImageNet-1K, our Foldnet-S1 achieves 79.0% Top-1 accuracy, while only has 1.0 ms inference speed on mobile devices. Comprehensive experimental results on high-resolution downstream tasks show that FoldNet outperforms existing efficient models, maintaining a superior trade-off between inference speed and accuracy. Published: 2026-05-06T19:42:39+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ang Li; Shouxiang Ni

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3690984

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Traditional efficient CNNs face limitations in capturing global dependencies due to local receptive fields, while ViTs suffer from quadratic complexity. Recent works attempt to combine convolution and attention mechanisms, yet still struggle with computational overhead that limits inference speed on mobile devices. In this work, we propose FoldNet, a pure convolutional architecture that achieves global context representation through hardware-efficient operations. First, we propose the Fold Convolution that reorganizes spatial pixels into the channel dimension, capturing global spatial attention via channel interactions, with lower computational costs compared to ViTs. Second, we design the Partial Re-parameterization Module that applies the structural re-parameterization technique to a subset of feature channels. This approach achieves nearly identical performance compared to full-channel processing while significantly minimizing redundant computations and memory accesses. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of FoldNet over existing state-of-the-art methods. For example, on ImageNet-1K, our Foldnet-S1 achieves 79.0% Top-1 accuracy, while only has 1.0 ms inference speed on mobile devices. Comprehensive experimental results on high-resolution downstream tasks show that FoldNet outperforms existing efficient models, maintaining a superior trade-off between inference speed and accuracy.

item.title: D <sup>2</sup> FT-Net: Frequency-Spatial Dual Domain Fine-Tuning of Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Domain Generalization Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Zimeng Shen
Jue Wang
Tianyu Wei
Wenchao Liu
Xiaoyan Lu
Liang Chen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: In recent years, remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation has attracted increasing attention due to significant domain shifts caused by variations in sensors, imaging conditions, and geographic regions. Vision foundation models (VFMs) possess strong general-purpose feature extraction capabilities and can be effectively transferred across diverse data, showing great potential for remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation. However, existing VFM-based spatial domain parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods struggle to handle pronounced cross-domain intra-class variations in remote sensing. To address this issue, the Adaptive Frequency Aware Adapter (AFA-Adapter) is proposed, which adaptively selects frequency components to improve cross-domain intra-class feature consistency. Building upon this, the Spatial Multi-Prototype Adapter (SMP-Adapter) is proposed, which clusters multiple prototypes for features of each land-cover category to model complex intra-class diversity. Land-cover features are then weighted by their nearest intra-class prototype, thereby enhancing the discriminability of easily confused features at feature cluster boundaries. By integrating these two modules, we propose a Frequency–Spatial Dual Domain Fine-Tuning Network (D2FT-Net), which effectively alleviates cross-domain intra-class variations and improves the generalization capability of VFMs for remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation. Extensive experiments under four cross-domain settings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed D2FT-Net, which achieves an average mIoU improvement of 1.09% over state-of-the-art methods, with the best gain reaching 1.64%. The source code will be released at https://github.com/ssshen0315/D2FT-Net. Published: 2026-05-07T19:53:06+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zimeng Shen; Jue Wang; Tianyu Wei; Wenchao Liu; Xiaoyan Lu; Liang Chen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3691111

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

In recent years, remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation has attracted increasing attention due to significant domain shifts caused by variations in sensors, imaging conditions, and geographic regions. Vision foundation models (VFMs) possess strong general-purpose feature extraction capabilities and can be effectively transferred across diverse data, showing great potential for remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation. However, existing VFM-based spatial domain parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods struggle to handle pronounced cross-domain intra-class variations in remote sensing. To address this issue, the Adaptive Frequency Aware Adapter (AFA-Adapter) is proposed, which adaptively selects frequency components to improve cross-domain intra-class feature consistency. Building upon this, the Spatial Multi-Prototype Adapter (SMP-Adapter) is proposed, which clusters multiple prototypes for features of each land-cover category to model complex intra-class diversity. Land-cover features are then weighted by their nearest intra-class prototype, thereby enhancing the discriminability of easily confused features at feature cluster boundaries. By integrating these two modules, we propose a Frequency–Spatial Dual Domain Fine-Tuning Network (D2FT-Net), which effectively alleviates cross-domain intra-class variations and improves the generalization capability of VFMs for remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation. Extensive experiments under four cross-domain settings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed D2FT-Net, which achieves an average mIoU improvement of 1.09% over state-of-the-art methods, with the best gain reaching 1.64%. The source code will be released at https://github.com/ssshen0315/D2FT-Net.

item.title: Infrared and Visible Image Fusion Using Bimodal Neuron and Dynamic Receptive Field Mechanisms
item.dc:creator: Shaobing Gao
Minjie Tan
Shun Lv
Yiguang Liu
Yongjie Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) significantly enhances scene interpretation by integrating broad-spectrum information. Drawing inspiration from specific snakes that possess an evolutionarily optimized bimodal sensory system capable of parallel processing infrared and visible radiation, we propose a novel IVIF framework incorporating two key elements: nonlinear cross-modal interactions across six distinct classes of snake bimodal neurons and dynamic center-surround receptive field organization. These biological principles are mathematically formalized and integrated within a deep neural network (DNN), optimized through an object detection region-guided loss and a frequency-dependent fusion loss that enable data-driven fusion strategy learning. Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized model effectively emulates the infrared-visible information integration observed in snake bimodal neurons. Critically, the nonlinear bimodal neurons capture a significantly greater amount of edge information and finer mid-to-high-frequency details, which are essential for the subsequent reconstruction of the fused image. Furthermore, a comprehensive evaluation of visual quality, encompassing both qualitative and quantitative assessments on six datasets, along with extensive object detection and semantic segmentation experiments using the fused images in both daytime and nighttime scenarios, demonstrates that our model outperforms traditional biologically-inspired IVIF algorithms, achieving performance comparable to SOTA DNN-based methods. Published: 2026-05-06T19:43:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shaobing Gao; Minjie Tan; Shun Lv; Yiguang Liu; Yongjie Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3689405

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) significantly enhances scene interpretation by integrating broad-spectrum information. Drawing inspiration from specific snakes that possess an evolutionarily optimized bimodal sensory system capable of parallel processing infrared and visible radiation, we propose a novel IVIF framework incorporating two key elements: nonlinear cross-modal interactions across six distinct classes of snake bimodal neurons and dynamic center-surround receptive field organization. These biological principles are mathematically formalized and integrated within a deep neural network (DNN), optimized through an object detection region-guided loss and a frequency-dependent fusion loss that enable data-driven fusion strategy learning. Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized model effectively emulates the infrared-visible information integration observed in snake bimodal neurons. Critically, the nonlinear bimodal neurons capture a significantly greater amount of edge information and finer mid-to-high-frequency details, which are essential for the subsequent reconstruction of the fused image. Furthermore, a comprehensive evaluation of visual quality, encompassing both qualitative and quantitative assessments on six datasets, along with extensive object detection and semantic segmentation experiments using the fused images in both daytime and nighttime scenarios, demonstrates that our model outperforms traditional biologically-inspired IVIF algorithms, achieving performance comparable to SOTA DNN-based methods.

item.title: ADANet: Adversarial Distribution Alignment Network for Multi-view Semi-supervised Classification
item.dc:creator: Sujia Huang
Lele Fu
Zhaoliang Chen
Tong Zhang
Xiaoli Li
Zhen Cui
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Multi-view learning aims to integrate multi-source information for a comprehensive data representation, which has gained widespread attention in image processing. Each view contains view-specific noise and joint features associated with other views, and thus exploring the specificity and consistency among views is a typical solution to deal with multi-view data for learning discriminative representations. In this paper, we present a theory-induced model, termed Adversarial Distribution Alignment Network (ADANet), which learns view-invariant features and alleviate the negative impact of view-specific noise. We first demonstrate the necessity of suppressing view-specific noise and capturing view-invariant features inspired by the theory of view generalization, and then derive two collaborative modules: a feature disentangler and an adversarial alignment module. In detail, the feature disentanglement separates view-specific noise and view-invariant features by minimizing the mutual information between them. Following this, a negative entropy is proposed to suppress the negative impact of view-specific noise. Meanwhile, the adversarial module uses the adversarial technique that can fit more complex data conformed to different distributions to adaptively align cross-view features so that features encoded in different views converge. Substantial experiments are constructed on multi-view datasets, demonstrating that ADANet can achieve more promising performance compared to other superior methods. Code is available at https://github.com/huangsuj/ADANet. Published: 2026-05-06T19:43:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sujia Huang; Lele Fu; Zhaoliang Chen; Tong Zhang; Xiaoli Li; Zhen Cui

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3689430

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Multi-view learning aims to integrate multi-source information for a comprehensive data representation, which has gained widespread attention in image processing. Each view contains view-specific noise and joint features associated with other views, and thus exploring the specificity and consistency among views is a typical solution to deal with multi-view data for learning discriminative representations. In this paper, we present a theory-induced model, termed Adversarial Distribution Alignment Network (ADANet), which learns view-invariant features and alleviate the negative impact of view-specific noise. We first demonstrate the necessity of suppressing view-specific noise and capturing view-invariant features inspired by the theory of view generalization, and then derive two collaborative modules: a feature disentangler and an adversarial alignment module. In detail, the feature disentanglement separates view-specific noise and view-invariant features by minimizing the mutual information between them. Following this, a negative entropy is proposed to suppress the negative impact of view-specific noise. Meanwhile, the adversarial module uses the adversarial technique that can fit more complex data conformed to different distributions to adaptively align cross-view features so that features encoded in different views converge. Substantial experiments are constructed on multi-view datasets, demonstrating that ADANet can achieve more promising performance compared to other superior methods. Code is available at https://github.com/huangsuj/ADANet.

item.title: From Weak to Strong: Cross-level Energy Field Aggregation for Infrared Small Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Jinfeng Fang
Zhixin Ma
Zhenwei Zhang
Guanglei Song
Haiting Tan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains a challenging task due to the lack of discriminative texture cues in targets and the presence of highly complex backgrounds. Inspired by the spectral energy distribution characteristics inherent to infrared imagery, this paper develops a novel Cross-layer Energy Field Aggregation Network (CEFANet), which focuses on target energy discontinuities embedded in cluttered backgrounds to achieve dynamic discrimination and feature enhancement of small targets with varying states. Specifically, to effectively exploit the fact that infrared targets typically manifest as high-energy responses at local scales, we introduce an Energy Field Attention (EFA) module that selectively amplifies target energy mutations while suppressing background noise. In addition, we propose a Cross-layer State Fusion (CSF) module based on channel-wise state awareness and adaptive weighting mechanisms, enabling precise modulation of low-level features under the guidance of high-level semantic states. Extensive experiments conducted on multiple public benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed CEFANet significantly improves the detection performance of infrared small targets. Published: 2026-05-07T19:56:24+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinfeng Fang; Zhixin Ma; Zhenwei Zhang; Guanglei Song; Haiting Tan

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3691152

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains a challenging task due to the lack of discriminative texture cues in targets and the presence of highly complex backgrounds. Inspired by the spectral energy distribution characteristics inherent to infrared imagery, this paper develops a novel Cross-layer Energy Field Aggregation Network (CEFANet), which focuses on target energy discontinuities embedded in cluttered backgrounds to achieve dynamic discrimination and feature enhancement of small targets with varying states. Specifically, to effectively exploit the fact that infrared targets typically manifest as high-energy responses at local scales, we introduce an Energy Field Attention (EFA) module that selectively amplifies target energy mutations while suppressing background noise. In addition, we propose a Cross-layer State Fusion (CSF) module based on channel-wise state awareness and adaptive weighting mechanisms, enabling precise modulation of low-level features under the guidance of high-level semantic states. Extensive experiments conducted on multiple public benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed CEFANet significantly improves the detection performance of infrared small targets.

item.title: Hyperspectral object tracking with dimensionality reduction and spatial–spectral–temporal modeling
item.dc:creator: Zhuanfeng Li
Jing Wang
Jue Zhang
Diqi Chen
Guanyiman Fu
Jiangtao Wang
Jianfeng Lu
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Hyperspectral object tracking significantly outperforms traditional color video-based tracking due to its material identification ability enabled by rich spectral bands. However, existing hyperspectral object tracking methods suffer from two critical limitations: the difficulty of learning discriminative features due to spectral band redundancy, and the challenge of fully modeling spatial–spectral–temporal (SST) dependencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel video-based hyperspectral tracking framework, termed DRSST-Net, which integrates a dimensionality reduction module with a context-aware SST modeling module. The dimensionality reduction module employs a nonlinear, data-driven weighting strategy that integrates an intra-band discrimination block and a cross-band weighting block. This design suppresses spectral redundancy while enhancing heterogeneity, thereby improving material discrimination ability. The context-aware SST modeling module integrates spatial–spectral features with temporal contextual information by combining the Mamba architecture with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism. The Mamba is employed to capture temporal dependencies, while the MoE dynamically activates task-relevant experts to enhance adaptability across diverse scenes. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets, e.g., HOT2020, HOT2022, IMEC25, and MUST, demonstrate that our DRSST-Net achieves state-of-the-art tracking performance, improving tracking accuracy and robustness. The source code will be available at https://github.com/hscv/DRSST-Net . Published: 2026-05-06T23:07:32+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhuanfeng Li; Jing Wang; Jue Zhang; Diqi Chen; Guanyiman Fu; Jiangtao Wang; Jianfeng Lu

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.052

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

Hyperspectral object tracking significantly outperforms traditional color video-based tracking due to its material identification ability enabled by rich spectral bands. However, existing hyperspectral object tracking methods suffer from two critical limitations: the difficulty of learning discriminative features due to spectral band redundancy, and the challenge of fully modeling spatial–spectral–temporal (SST) dependencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel video-based hyperspectral tracking framework, termed DRSST-Net, which integrates a dimensionality reduction module with a context-aware SST modeling module. The dimensionality reduction module employs a nonlinear, data-driven weighting strategy that integrates an intra-band discrimination block and a cross-band weighting block. This design suppresses spectral redundancy while enhancing heterogeneity, thereby improving material discrimination ability. The context-aware SST modeling module integrates spatial–spectral features with temporal contextual information by combining the Mamba architecture with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism. The Mamba is employed to capture temporal dependencies, while the MoE dynamically activates task-relevant experts to enhance adaptability across diverse scenes. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets, e.g., HOT2020, HOT2022, IMEC25, and MUST, demonstrate that our DRSST-Net achieves state-of-the-art tracking performance, improving tracking accuracy and robustness. The source code will be available at https://github.com/hscv/DRSST-Net .

item.title: Zero-Shot Neural Network Evaluation with Sample-Wise Activation Patterns
item.dc:creator: Yameng Peng
Andy Song
Haytham M. Fayek
Vic Ciesielski
Xiaojun Chang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Zero-shot proxies, also known as training-free metrics, are widely adopted to reduce the computational overhead in neural network evaluation for scenarios such as Neural Architecture Search (NAS), as they do not require any training. Existing zero-shot metrics have several limitations, including weak correlation with the true performance and poor generalisation across different networks or downstream tasks. For example, most of these metrics apply only to either convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or Transformers, but not both. To address these limitations, we propose Sample-Wise Activation Patterns (SWAP), and its derivative, SWAP-Score, a novel and highly effective zero-shot metric. SWAP-Score is broadly applicable across both architecture families and task domains, demonstrating strong predictive performance in the majority of tasks. This metric measures the expressivity of neural networks over a mini-batch of samples, showing a high correlation with the neural networks' ground-truth performance. For both CNNs and Transformers, the SWAP-Score outperforms existing zero-shot metrics across computer vision and natural language processing tasks. For instance, Spearman's correlation coefficient between the SWAP-Score and CIFAR-10 validation accuracy for DARTS CNNs is 0.93, and 0.71 for FlexiBERT Transformers on GLUE tasks. Moreover, SWAP-Score is label-independent, hence can be applied at the pre-training stage of language models to estimate their performance for downstream tasks. When applied to NAS, SWAP-empowered NAS, SWAP-NAS can achieve competitive performance using only approximately 6 and 9 minutes of GPU time, on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet respectively. Our code is available at: https://github.com/pym1024/SWAP_Universal. Published: 2026-05-07T19:53:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yameng Peng; Andy Song; Haytham M. Fayek; Vic Ciesielski; Xiaojun Chang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3691075

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Zero-shot proxies, also known as training-free metrics, are widely adopted to reduce the computational overhead in neural network evaluation for scenarios such as Neural Architecture Search (NAS), as they do not require any training. Existing zero-shot metrics have several limitations, including weak correlation with the true performance and poor generalisation across different networks or downstream tasks. For example, most of these metrics apply only to either convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or Transformers, but not both. To address these limitations, we propose Sample-Wise Activation Patterns (SWAP), and its derivative, SWAP-Score, a novel and highly effective zero-shot metric. SWAP-Score is broadly applicable across both architecture families and task domains, demonstrating strong predictive performance in the majority of tasks. This metric measures the expressivity of neural networks over a mini-batch of samples, showing a high correlation with the neural networks' ground-truth performance. For both CNNs and Transformers, the SWAP-Score outperforms existing zero-shot metrics across computer vision and natural language processing tasks. For instance, Spearman's correlation coefficient between the SWAP-Score and CIFAR-10 validation accuracy for DARTS CNNs is 0.93, and 0.71 for FlexiBERT Transformers on GLUE tasks. Moreover, SWAP-Score is label-independent, hence can be applied at the pre-training stage of language models to estimate their performance for downstream tasks. When applied to NAS, SWAP-empowered NAS, SWAP-NAS can achieve competitive performance using only approximately 6 and 9 minutes of GPU time, on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet respectively. Our code is available at: https://github.com/pym1024/SWAP_Universal.

item.title: Dataset Distillation via a Noise-Unconstrained Generative Model
item.dc:creator: Jingxuan Zhang
Lei Dai
Fei Ye
Zhihua Chen
Ping Li
Xiaokang Yang
Bin Sheng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Dataset distillation (DD) aims to synthesize a more compact dataset than the original one and models trained on it are expected to have the same generalization capabilities as on the original dataset. Previous work via a generative model (GM) faces several limitations. First, GM struggles to generate representative samples due to a lack of constraints. Second, it overlooks the relationships between generated samples, limiting its effectiveness. In this paper, a new noise-unconstrained GM-based DD framework is proposed. In the distillation stage, an adaptive matching coefficient is introduced to align generated images with representative class elements and the MiniMax loss function is extended to reduce the optimization difficulty. In the deployment stage, features among each generative image are ensembled by gradient-matching based DD. Theoretical analysis based on McDiarmid's inequality demonstrates that the proposed components can reduce the generalization error of the original baseline method. We also provide insights into the potential of generated images as an effective proxy dataset for DD. For example, on the ImageWoof dataset with 50 distilled images per class using a 6-layer ConvNet for evaluation, generated images outperform 25%, 50%, and 75% original images by 8.4%, 6.3%, and 8.3% in distillation performance. Our method effectively handles both low- and high-resolution datasets, with experiments on 11 benchmarks demonstrating its efficacy. Published: 2026-05-06T19:40:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jingxuan Zhang; Lei Dai; Fei Ye; Zhihua Chen; Ping Li; Xiaokang Yang; Bin Sheng

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3690778

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Dataset distillation (DD) aims to synthesize a more compact dataset than the original one and models trained on it are expected to have the same generalization capabilities as on the original dataset. Previous work via a generative model (GM) faces several limitations. First, GM struggles to generate representative samples due to a lack of constraints. Second, it overlooks the relationships between generated samples, limiting its effectiveness. In this paper, a new noise-unconstrained GM-based DD framework is proposed. In the distillation stage, an adaptive matching coefficient is introduced to align generated images with representative class elements and the MiniMax loss function is extended to reduce the optimization difficulty. In the deployment stage, features among each generative image are ensembled by gradient-matching based DD. Theoretical analysis based on McDiarmid's inequality demonstrates that the proposed components can reduce the generalization error of the original baseline method. We also provide insights into the potential of generated images as an effective proxy dataset for DD. For example, on the ImageWoof dataset with 50 distilled images per class using a 6-layer ConvNet for evaluation, generated images outperform 25%, 50%, and 75% original images by 8.4%, 6.3%, and 8.3% in distillation performance. Our method effectively handles both low- and high-resolution datasets, with experiments on 11 benchmarks demonstrating its efficacy.

item.title: Scattering-Clutter Disentangled Prototypes for SAR Open Set Recognition
item.dc:creator: Xuxiang He
Zheyuan Cai
Jingming Sun
Jieyuan Yang
Qiang Cheng
Yue Huang
Xinghao Ding
Yuhao Yang
Xiaotong Tu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) systems face significant challenges in Open Set Recognition (OSR) due to the complex coupling between target scattering and background clutter, as well as the severe appearance variations induced by aspect changes, inevitably leading to ambiguous decision boundaries in OSR. To address these issues, we propose a Scattering-Clutter Disentangled Prototypes Network (SCDP-Net). First, we design a dual-stream architecture with physics-aware preprocessing to separate scattering-dominant representations from clutter-related representations. Second, to handle aspect-dependent variations, we introduce a multi-view prototype learning mechanism that models the diverse scattering patterns of targets within a compact feature space. Furthermore, a test-time adaptation strategy is developed to dynamically calibrate the decision boundary for unknown classes during inference. Experiments on the large-scale ATRNet-STAR dataset demonstrate that SCDP-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving AUROC by 5.27% and 3.26% under standard and extended operating conditions, respectively. Published: 2026-05-07T19:56:24+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xuxiang He; Zheyuan Cai; Jingming Sun; Jieyuan Yang; Qiang Cheng; Yue Huang; Xinghao Ding; Yuhao Yang; Xiaotong Tu

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3691105

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) systems face significant challenges in Open Set Recognition (OSR) due to the complex coupling between target scattering and background clutter, as well as the severe appearance variations induced by aspect changes, inevitably leading to ambiguous decision boundaries in OSR. To address these issues, we propose a Scattering-Clutter Disentangled Prototypes Network (SCDP-Net). First, we design a dual-stream architecture with physics-aware preprocessing to separate scattering-dominant representations from clutter-related representations. Second, to handle aspect-dependent variations, we introduce a multi-view prototype learning mechanism that models the diverse scattering patterns of targets within a compact feature space. Furthermore, a test-time adaptation strategy is developed to dynamically calibrate the decision boundary for unknown classes during inference. Experiments on the large-scale ATRNet-STAR dataset demonstrate that SCDP-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving AUROC by 5.27% and 3.26% under standard and extended operating conditions, respectively.

item.title: Open Set Domain Adaptation via Target-relaxed Optimal Transport
item.dc:creator: Chuan-Xian Ren
Zi-Xian Huang
Hong Yan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Open set domain adaptation (OSDA) aims to transfer classification-oriented knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain, which faces the challenges from unseen knowledge in open-set scenarios, i.e., unknown classes privileged to the target domain. Existing methods usually identify unknown classes from classifier prediction directly, which are sensitive to the intrinsic clustering structure and cluster numbers of the unknown class data. In this paper, inspired by the sample relation characterization ability of Optimal Transport (OT), we propose a new type of OT method for OSDA, namely, Target-relaxed Optimal Transport (TROT). Compared with existing OT with strict marginal constraints, TROT imposes a single-side relaxation to the mass requirement on the open-set target domain. Theoretically, we prove that such a relaxation can reduce mis-matches between known and unknown classes, which indicates the transport plan of TROT is promising to identify unknown classes. Methodologically, TROT can identify unknown classes adaptively and map the cross-domain shared data with a sparse plan assignment, which improves both the effectiveness and robustness of known class alignment; besides, a graph embedding with multi-cluster structure of unknown classes is designed to learn a discriminative metric space for open-set classification. Empirically, extensive evaluations are conducted on several image datasets, where TROT achieves significant performance improvements compared with existing techniques for visual recognition in open-set scenarios. Published: 2026-05-06T19:43:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chuan-Xian Ren; Zi-Xian Huang; Hong Yan

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3689416

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Open set domain adaptation (OSDA) aims to transfer classification-oriented knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain, which faces the challenges from unseen knowledge in open-set scenarios, i.e., unknown classes privileged to the target domain. Existing methods usually identify unknown classes from classifier prediction directly, which are sensitive to the intrinsic clustering structure and cluster numbers of the unknown class data. In this paper, inspired by the sample relation characterization ability of Optimal Transport (OT), we propose a new type of OT method for OSDA, namely, Target-relaxed Optimal Transport (TROT). Compared with existing OT with strict marginal constraints, TROT imposes a single-side relaxation to the mass requirement on the open-set target domain. Theoretically, we prove that such a relaxation can reduce mis-matches between known and unknown classes, which indicates the transport plan of TROT is promising to identify unknown classes. Methodologically, TROT can identify unknown classes adaptively and map the cross-domain shared data with a sparse plan assignment, which improves both the effectiveness and robustness of known class alignment; besides, a graph embedding with multi-cluster structure of unknown classes is designed to learn a discriminative metric space for open-set classification. Empirically, extensive evaluations are conducted on several image datasets, where TROT achieves significant performance improvements compared with existing techniques for visual recognition in open-set scenarios.

item.title: Towards the Connection between Activation Sparsity and Flat Minima
item.dc:creator: Ze Peng
Jian Zhang
Lei Qi
Yang Gao
Yinghuan Shi
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: The observation that activation sparsity emerges in MLP blocks of standardly trained Transformers offers an opportunity to drastically reduce computation costs without sacrificing performance. To theoretically explain this phenomenon, existing works have shown that activation sparsity does not result from the data properties or data fitting but from the implicit bias of the training process. However, these connections are obtained with strong assumptions (e.g., shallow networks, a small number of training steps, and special training techniques), which cannot be applied to deep models standardly trained with a large number of steps. Different from these works, we find that the flatness of loss landscapes is also closely related to the MLP activation sparsity and can serve as a weaker assumption because it naturally emerges in the standard training of deep networks without the above strong assumptions. Specifically, we find that 1) the MLP activation sparsity equals a ratio between “augmented f latness” (a weighted sum of flatness measures) and the product of the input norm and activation gradient of the MLP. We empirically find that this ratio decreases during training, leading to sparse activations. 2) We also propose the notion of derivative sparsity, which reduces to activation sparsity under ReLU, but further enables pruning in the backward propagation and is more stable than activation sparsity. With the theoretical findings, we can further encourage activation sparsity by decreasing the numerator and increasing the denominator of the ratio: 1) To improve (lower) the flatness, we add different bias vectors to input tokens of MLP blocks to strengthen stochastic gradient noises that drive the model to a flat area. 2) We restrict the lower bound of affine parameters in LayerNorm to increase the input norm of MLPs. 3) To increase the activation sparsity, we propose an activation function JSReLU to encourage the search of parameters with sparse derivatives and sparse activations. These plug-and-play modifications can effectively reduce the ratio and produce sparser activations. Experiments on ImageNet-1K and C4 demonstrate relative improvements of at least 36% on inference sparsity and at least 50% on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers, indicating further potential cost reduction in both inference and training. Published: 2026-05-07T19:53:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ze Peng; Jian Zhang; Lei Qi; Yang Gao; Yinghuan Shi

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3691379

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

The observation that activation sparsity emerges in MLP blocks of standardly trained Transformers offers an opportunity to drastically reduce computation costs without sacrificing performance. To theoretically explain this phenomenon, existing works have shown that activation sparsity does not result from the data properties or data fitting but from the implicit bias of the training process. However, these connections are obtained with strong assumptions (e.g., shallow networks, a small number of training steps, and special training techniques), which cannot be applied to deep models standardly trained with a large number of steps. Different from these works, we find that the flatness of loss landscapes is also closely related to the MLP activation sparsity and can serve as a weaker assumption because it naturally emerges in the standard training of deep networks without the above strong assumptions. Specifically, we find that 1) the MLP activation sparsity equals a ratio between “augmented f latness” (a weighted sum of flatness measures) and the product of the input norm and activation gradient of the MLP. We empirically find that this ratio decreases during training, leading to sparse activations. 2) We also propose the notion of derivative sparsity, which reduces to activation sparsity under ReLU, but further enables pruning in the backward propagation and is more stable than activation sparsity. With the theoretical findings, we can further encourage activation sparsity by decreasing the numerator and increasing the denominator of the ratio: 1) To improve (lower) the flatness, we add different bias vectors to input tokens of MLP blocks to strengthen stochastic gradient noises that drive the model to a flat area. 2) We restrict the lower bound of affine parameters in LayerNorm to increase the input norm of MLPs. 3) To increase the activation sparsity, we propose an activation function JSReLU to encourage the search of parameters with sparse derivatives and sparse activations. These plug-and-play modifications can effectively reduce the ratio and produce sparser activations. Experiments on ImageNet-1K and C4 demonstrate relative improvements of at least 36% on inference sparsity and at least 50% on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers, indicating further potential cost reduction in both inference and training.

item.title: DASR+: Training Domain Distance Aware Network for Unsupervised Image Super-Resolution
item.dc:creator: Xiaorui Zhao
Yunxuan Wei
Xin Deng
Yawei Li
Radu Timofte
Hengjie Song
Shuhang Gu
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Recently, unsupervised super-resolution has attracted increasing attention due to its promising potential in real-world applications. Early studies proposed generating synthetic low-resolution (LR) images that share similar degradation characteristics with real LR images while preserving the content of high-resolution (HR) images, and utilizing the generated pseudo pairs for supervised training of super-resolution networks. Nevertheless, the SR performance of these approaches is limited by the inevitable domain gap between synthetic and real LR images. In this paper, we propose a novel domain distance aware super-resolution (DASR+) approach with newly designed domain gap aware training strategy, domain distance weighted supervision strategy, and a domain distance adaptive network for unsupervised image super-resolution. Particularly, domain gap aware training takes additional benefit from real LR images and performs feature-level as well as image-level adversarial learning to leverage real-world LR images in the target domain. In addition, domain distance weighted supervision enables a more rational use of generated LR-HR pairs using domain distance information, and the domain distance adaptive SR network injects domain distance information into the network architecture to adaptively adjust the SR mapping. As a result, the SR model trained on synthetic data can be generalized to super-resolve real-world LR images without corresponding ground-truth images. We evaluate DASR+ on both synthetic and real datasets, our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised SR methods in generating results with more realistic textures. Published: 2026-05-07T19:35:06+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaorui Zhao; Yunxuan Wei; Xin Deng; Yawei Li; Radu Timofte; Hengjie Song; Shuhang Gu

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02822-2

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Recently, unsupervised super-resolution has attracted increasing attention due to its promising potential in real-world applications. Early studies proposed generating synthetic low-resolution (LR) images that share similar degradation characteristics with real LR images while preserving the content of high-resolution (HR) images, and utilizing the generated pseudo pairs for supervised training of super-resolution networks. Nevertheless, the SR performance of these approaches is limited by the inevitable domain gap between synthetic and real LR images. In this paper, we propose a novel domain distance aware super-resolution (DASR+) approach with newly designed domain gap aware training strategy, domain distance weighted supervision strategy, and a domain distance adaptive network for unsupervised image super-resolution. Particularly, domain gap aware training takes additional benefit from real LR images and performs feature-level as well as image-level adversarial learning to leverage real-world LR images in the target domain. In addition, domain distance weighted supervision enables a more rational use of generated LR-HR pairs using domain distance information, and the domain distance adaptive SR network injects domain distance information into the network architecture to adaptively adjust the SR mapping. As a result, the SR model trained on synthetic data can be generalized to super-resolve real-world LR images without corresponding ground-truth images. We evaluate DASR+ on both synthetic and real datasets, our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised SR methods in generating results with more realistic textures.

item.title: Hierarchical Frequency-Spatial Integration Network for Remote Sensing Super-Resolution
item.dc:creator: Yunong Chen
Zhenfeng Shao
Josue Anonio Nescolarde Selva
John Van Genderen
Deren Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing image super-resolution offers a cost-effective and efficient approach to obtain high-resolution imagery, playing a crucial role in enhancing the precision of image analysis. However, existing methods suffer from an inherent trade-off between performance and efficiency, and their over-reliance on low-level features leads to insufficient fusion of multi-scale information, low detail fidelity in reconstructed results, along with overfitting and artifacts. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Hierarchical Frequency-Spatial Interaction Network (HiFi-Net) for remote sensing image super-resolution. The proposed HiFi-Net incorporates a frequency-spatial attention module that leverages Fourier transformation to efficiently model global dependencies, thereby enhancing perceptual scope while reducing computational complexity. Furthermore, HiFi-Net employs a hierarchical interaction mechanism, comprised of a hierarchical spatial cross-attention module and a hierarchical channel cross-attention module, to facilitate cross-level feature modulation. This design effectively integrates low-level high-frequency details with high-level features, thereby adaptively aggregating critical information and resulting in superior super-resolution performance. Extensive experiments on several public remote sensing datasets demonstrate that HiFi-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative metrics and visual quality. Published: 2026-05-06T19:40:58+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.794 (must_read)
